coredata.dc:title: A one-pot strategy for preparation of carboxymethyl xylan-based hydrogels with antibacterial property
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Zhenhua
Fan, Xiaotong
Xiang, Zhouyang
Wang, Luliang
Wang, Shengdan
He, Chao
Zhang, Xiaoran
Liu, Yushen
coredata.dc:description: Hemicellulose-based hydrogels has been drawn more and more attention in recent years. In this paper, a series of carboxymethyl xylan-based hydrogels consisting carboxymethyl xylan (CMX), acrylamide (AM) and ε-poly-(L-lysine) carbon dot (PL-CD) were synthesized by one-pot strategy via free radical polymerization. The N, N′-methylenebis(acrylamide) (MBA) was used as the crosslinking agent to form networks of the hydrogels. The PL-CD was used as the antibacterial agent and nano-reinforcing agent to improve the antibacterial properties and mechanical properties of the hydrogels. The contents of AM, crosslinking agent and PL-CD had obvious effects on the compressive strength, swelling ratio (SR) and antibacterial properties. With the increase of AM, MBA and PL-CD contents, the mechanical properties were improved. The SEM spectra confirmed the porous structure of the hydrogels. Furthermore, the pH-responsive behavior was also investigated by SR.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glucuronoxylans
Hydrogel
Antibacterial property
Carbon dot

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The FT-IR spectra of glucuronoxylans, CMX and PL-CD.
The fluorescence spectra of the PL-CD.
FT-IR spectra of MBA-4, MBA-5, AM-2, PL-CD-3 and CMX.
The XRD pattern of CMX, MBA-5 and PL-CD-3.
The compressive strength of the PL-CD-CMX-PAM hydrogels.
Effects of MBA, AM and PL-CD on swelling ratio (SR) of the PL-CD-CMX-PAM hydrogels at different pH (A, B, C) and in water (D, E, F).
The TGA of MBA-5 hydrogel 4, AM-1 hydrogel and PL-CD-3 hydrogel.
SEM images of MBA-5 hydrogel (A, B), AM-1 hydrogel (C, D) and PL-CD-3 hydrogel (E, F) at 200 µm and 100 µm.
Photograph of inhibition zones of PL-CD@ODA hydrogels against S. aureus.
The preparation condition of PL-CD-CMX-PAM hydrogels.
The diameter of the inhibition zone.
Comparation of PL-CD-CMX-PAM hydrogel and the hydrogel without PL-CD.

row.entry: The diameter of the inhibition zone (mm)

head.ce:title: A one-pot strategy for preparation of carboxymethyl xylan-based hydrogels with antibacterial property

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Engineering, Ludong University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hemicellulose-based hydrogels has been drawn more and more attention in recent years. In this paper, a series of carboxymethyl xylan-based hydrogels consisting carboxymethyl xylan (CMX), acrylamide (AM) and ε-poly-(L-lysine) carbon dot (PL-CD) were synthesized by one-pot strategy via free radical polymerization. The N, N′-methylenebis(acrylamide) (MBA) was used as the crosslinking agent to form networks of the hydrogels. The PL-CD was used as the antibacterial agent and nano-reinforcing agent to improve the antibacterial properties and mechanical properties of the hydrogels. The contents of AM, crosslinking agent and PL-CD had obvious effects on the compressive strength, swelling ratio (SR) and antibacterial properties. With the increase of AM, MBA and PL-CD contents, the mechanical properties were improved. The SEM spectra confirmed the porous structure of the hydrogels. Furthermore, the pH-responsive behavior was also investigated by SR.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: During the progress of wound healing, the bacterial infection are always the biggest problems (Chen et al., 2023; Zhong et al., 2021). The existence of bacterial can promote the infection and can lead to bacterial biofilms which can accelerate the reproduction of bacterial (Yang et al., 2023). Hydrogels are three-dimensional network polymers which can retain water without dissolving (Bercea et al., 2019). Hydrogels are widely used in wound healing, biomedicine and other industries (Forero-Doria et al., 2020; Peng et al., 2012a; Shen et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2023). Antibacterial hydrogels can prevent the infection. However, many raw materials of the hydrogel are obtained from petroleum-based resources, which could lead to poor biodegradability and poor biocompatibility (Zhong et al., 2021). Therefore, it is essential to develop bio-based antibacterial hydrogels.

In the several past decades, finding for the substitution of petroleum-based products is of great importance because of the environmental problems and the limited supply of petroleum-based products (Huo et al., 2022; Kundu et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019a). In order to solve this problem, the utilization of natural polymers in hydrogels are attracting considerable attention. Among these natural polymers-based hydrogels, polysaccharide-based hydrogels are widely used as their biological functions, biodegradability and low cost (Gao et al., 2016; Hu et al., 2023). Hemicellulose have been used to prepare hydrogels due to their low cost, biodegradability and abundant sources (Hu et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2023). The abundant hydroxyl groups of hemicellulose can be easily modified by chemical reactions, for example, etherification, cross-linking, esterification and oxidation (Amer et al., 2016; Hu et al., 2019; Peng et al., 2012b). However, the poor antibacterial properties and poor mechanical properties of hemicellulose limited their further applications. Therefore, it is necessary to obtain hemicellulose-based hydrogels with higher mechanical properties and antibacterial properties.

Nanocomposite hydrogels are composite hydrogels which contains nanoparticles and have high strength (Liu et al., 2019a). The nanoparticles can form organic/inorganic network that could distribute the stress, thus improving the material properties (Liu et al., 2019a). Some commonly used nanoparticles include metal nanoparticles, graphene, carbon nanotubes and so on (Sarwar et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2021). The nanoparticles can lead to higher strength enhancement of the hydrogels. However, many nanoparticles have poor antibacterial properties. In order to obtain high strength enhancement and antibacterial properties of nanocomposite hydrogels, it is necessary to find a nanoparticle with strength enhancement properties and antibacterial properties, which can avoid the extra additional antibacterial agents.

Among these nanoparticles, carbon dots (CDs) are carbon-based nanoparticles, usually < 10 nm in diameter (Monfared-Hajishirkiaee et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2021). In recent years, CDs have been used as anti-bacterial agents in biomedicine, food and other industries (Han et al., 2012; Li et al., 2020b). CDs can avoid the emergence of bacterial resistance due to their multiple anti-bacterial mechanisms (Li et al., 2020b). However, to best of our knowledge there are little literature using CDs to form hemicellulose-based hydrogels with antibacterial properties.

The aim of this work is to obtain carboxymethyl xylan-poly (acrylicamide) (CMX-PAM) hydrogels. It is hypothesized that the CDs could enhance the mechanical and antibacterial properties of the CMX-PAM hydrogels. The influence of CDs, acrylicamide and crosslinking agents were discussed on the mechanical and antibacterial properties.

Glucuronoxylans (M
w of 9482 g/mol). Acrylamide (AM) (AR, 99%), N, N′-methylenebis(acrylamide) (MBA) (AR, 99%), ammonium persulfate (APS) (AR, 98.5%), N, N, N′, N′-tetramethylethylenediamine (TMEDA) (AR, 99%), ε-poly-(L-lysine)(PL) (MW=2000–5000), sodium chloroacetate (SMCA) (AR, 98%), isopropyl alcohol (AR, ≥99.5%), sodium dihydrogen phosphate anhydrous (AR, 99%), sodium hydrogen phosphate anhydrous (AR, 99%), sodium hydroxide (AR, 95%) was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company Ltd (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid was obatined from Yantai Yuandong Fine Chemical Co. Ltd (Yantai, China).
ε-poly-(L-lysine) (2 g) was placed in a muffle furnace for 3 h (240 ℃). Then the samples were dialyzed (MwCO=5000 Da) for 24 h. At last, the PL-CDs were freeze-dried.

25 g glucuronoxylans was added to 125 mL NaOH solution (25%, w/w). Then 175 mL isopropyl alcohol were added and stirred (0.5 h). 21.84 g SMCA were added and the reaction was kept at 65 ℃ for 70 min. After reaction, the resulting solution was neutralized and precipitated with 95% ethanol (3 times volume). The precipitated was washed by 70% ethanol, and then lyophilized.

CMX (1 g) and PL-CDs (0.2–0.6 g) were added to 15 mL deionized water and stirred at 80 ℃ for 1 h. 10 wt% APS and 0.1 mL TEMDA were added to generated radicals. Subsequently, AM (2–10 g) and MBA (0.1–0.5 g) were added to deionized water (5 mL) and then added to the CMX-PL-CDs solution. The mixture was poured into the mould for 24 h at room temperature. Table. 1.

The degree of substitution (DS) of CMX was measured by titration methods (Hu et al., 2019). The DS was defined as the number of moles of SMCA reacted per mole of xylose units. Exactly 0.2 g CMX was added to deionized water (25 mL). The pH value was adjusted to 8.75 by alkali (0.03 M NaOH). Then, the pH was adjusted to 3.75 by acid (0.1 M HCl). The DS was calculated as follows:
(1)
DS =
132 × 0.1 ×
V
sample
−
V
blank
M − 80 × 0.1 ×
V
sample
−
V
blank
Where V
Sample and V
Blank is the consumption of HCl used to titrate sample and blank sample; M is the weight of the samples.
The FT-IR were determined by Nicolet Summit X spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher, USA) with a scanning range of 4000–500 cm−1. All the samples were ground and mixed with dried KBr.

The fluorescence spectroscopy was measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu RF-5301, Japan). The emission spectra were recorded in the wavelength range of 300–460 nm. The slit openings for both excitation and emission were fixed at 10 and 2.5 nm, respectively.

The dried hydrogels were placed in pH= 3.2. 7.2, 11.2 and distilled water, and the swelling was proceeded at 25 ℃ for 48 h. After 48 h, the hydrogels were weighed. The swelling ratio (SR) of the hydrogels was determined by the formula as follows:
(2)
SR
%
=
W
2
−
W
1
W
1
× 100 %
Where W
2 and W
1 is the weight after swelling for 48 h and the dried hydrogel.
The SEM images of the hydrogels were obtained by a scanning electron microscope (Zeiss EVO18, Germany). The hydrogels were freezing-dried before measurement.

The mechanical properties of hydrogels were determined by universal tensile machine (INSTRON 5565, USA), the compression speed was set at 50 mm/min. The hydrogel was cut in 3 × 3 × 3 cm before measurement.
XRD spectra was determined by using D8 Advance Bruker diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). The speed was set at 2°/ min and 2θ range was 5–60°.

The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed by using a Thermal analyzer (NETZSCH STA449 F3, Germany). The TGA tests were conducted from room temperature to 600 ℃ in N2. The samples were dried in an oven at 60 ℃ for 24 h before measurement.

To evaluate the antibacterial and inhibition activities against Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). The S. aureus were grown at 37 ℃ for one night. The prepared hydrogels were put into a disc and transferred to a LB agar plate covered with S. aureus suspension. The plates were incubated at 37 ℃ for one night, the bacterial inhibition area around the gel disc were measured.

The FT-IR spectra of glucuronoxylans, CMX and PL-CD are illustrated in Fig. 1. The absorbances between 500–1500 cm−1 were the typical bands of glucuronoxylans (Li et al., 2020a). A new peak at 1743 cm−1 of CMX was related to the typical absorption peak of carbonyl groups (Hu et al., 2023; Priyadarshi et al., 2020; Thakur et al., 2022). This result indicated that SMCA has been introduced onto the backbone of glucuronoxylans. The peak at 3419 cm−1 belonged to the -NH2 vibration, and the CO from amide stretching vibration appeared at 1668 cm−1 (Verma et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2021).

Fig. 2 shows the fluorescence spectra of PL-CD. The fluorescence intensity first increased when excitation wavelength increased, when the excitation wavelength increased upon 340 nm, the fluorescence intensity decreased. This result suggested that PL-CD have better fluorescence intensity at 320 nm and 340 nm. Furthermore, after pyrolysis, the ε-poly-(L-lysine) had transform into carbon dots and had fluorescent properties.

The FT-IR spectra of MBA-4, MBA-5, AM-2, PL-CD-3 and CMX are presented in Fig. 3. The bands at 3188 cm−1 and 1648 cm−1 were attributed to the presence of N-H and CO groups in AM and PL-CD, respectively (Kundu and Banerjee, 2019; Liu et al., 2019b; Peng et al., 2014). The peaks at 1527 cm−1, 1315 cm−1 and 896 cm−1 were the typical bands of glucuronoxylans (Ren et al., 2018). These results suggested that the AM monomer was grafted onto the backbone of CMX.

The XRD patterns of CMX, MBA-5 and PL-CD-3 are presented in Fig. 4. The diffraction peaks at 2θ= 9.1°, 11.0°, 12.3°, 19.0°, 22.6°, 24.9° and 31.6°, suggesting that CMX has the crystalline structure (Liu et al., 2019a). Moreover, the peaks of MBA-5 and PL-CD-3 at 20.7° became wider, responding to the amorphous structure. The changes of crystalline structure owing to the formation of the PL-CD-CMX-PAM hydrogels. These results confirmed the formation of the crystalline structure of the hydrogels.

Fig. 5 illustrates the stress-strain curves of PL-CD-CMX-PAM hydrogels. The compressive strength of MBA 1–5 was 63.0 KPa (MBA-1), 81.9 KPa (MBA-2), 148.8 KPa (MBA-3), 200.6 KPa (MBA-4) and 180.4 KPa (MBA-5). After crosslinking, the compressive strength increased from 63.0 KPa to 200.6 KPa, the strain increased from 53.4% to 57.3%. When the amount of MBA increased, the crosslinking density became larger, the compressive strength and strain were improved (Gao et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2019b). However, when MBA amount increased upon 0.4 g, the compressive strength and strain decreased from 200.6 KPa to 180.4 KPa and 57.3% to 50.3%. As the crosslinking density increased, the higher crosslinking density of the hydrogel network structure made it difficult to maintain the strength of the hydrogels (Peng et al., 2011). Hence, with the increasing of MBA amount, the compressive strength first increased and then decreased. Rising the AM amount from 2 g to 10 g led to an increase of compressive strength (from 131 KPa to 317.8 KPa) and strain (from 47.8% to 57.5%). The addition of AM can increase the crosslinking density which can increase the compressive strength. That is reason for that with the increasing of AM amounts, the compressive strength increased. When the amount of PL-CD increased, the compressive strength first increased and then decreased (Fig. 5 C). The hydrogen-bonding interaction between PL-CD and CMX, PAM improved the mechanical properties. However, when the amount of PL-CD increased upon 0.4 g, the compressive strength decreased from 158.7 KPa to 55.6 KPa. This could possibly be explained by that the PL-CD may aggregated and weaken the hydrogen bonds which could make the structure of hydrogels loose (Liu et al., 2019a; Priyadarshi et al., 2020). These results suggested that MBA, AM and PL-CD could improve the compressive strength while the amounts of MBA, AM and PL-CD should be kept at appropriate level.

The swelling ratio (SR) of the PL-CD-CMX-PAM hydrogels in water and at different pH are presented in Fig. 6. The SR of MBA-1–5 first increased and then decreased when the MBA amounts increased (Fig. 6 A and D). MBA-3 hydrogel had the highest SR in water and at different pH. When the MBA amounts increased from 0.1 g to 0.3 g, the cross-linking points increased which can facilitate the water absorption (Liu et al., 2019a). Furthermore, when the amount of MBA increased upon 0.3 g, the SR decreased as the cross-linking degree increased. The increasing of cross-linking points could reduce the storage space of water; then less water could be maintained in the hydrogels (Sun et al., 2013).

When the AM amounts increased, the SR first increased and then kept nearly stable. AM-4 hydrogel had the highest SR in water and at pH 11.2, while AM-5 hydrogel had the highest SR at pH 3.2 and pH 7.2. As the increasing of AM amounts led to higher availability of the AM molecules, more AM molecules can be grafted onto the backbone of the CMX, which could promote the formation of the network (Sun et al., 2013). This can be the reason for the increase of the SR. Furthermore, the hydrophilicity of the hydrogels could be enhanced due to the higher AM concentrations, and led to a higher absorption of water (Maleki et al., 2017; Peng et al., 2011).

With the increase of PL-CD amounts, the SR first increased and then kept stable. PL-CD-4 hydrogel had the highest SR among these samples. The PL-CD-5 hydrogel had the highest SR in water and at pH 11.2, while PL-CD-4 hydrogel had the highest SR at pH 3.2 and pH 7.2. The PL-CD could form hydrogen bonds with CMX and PAM which could promote the formation of the network structure, subsequently promoted the absorption of water (Yang et al., 2011). Moreover, when the amount of PL-CD increased upon 0.4 g, the SR kept stable. The hydrogen bonds made the network structure of hydrogels denser, which limited the capacity of the pores to hold water (Shen et al., 2020).

At pH 3.2, the -COOH groups of CMX could form inter- and intra-molecule hydrogen bonds with both -OH groups, the amido bonds and water (Peng et al., 2014; Yang et al., 2023). At pH 7.2, the hydrogen bonds were weakened. The SR decreased slightly with increasing the pH (Fig. 6A, B and C). At pH 11.2, the SR was much higher. At higher pH, the hydrogen bonds in the network were dissociated, and the electrostatic repulsion between the amido bonds made the PL-CD-CMX-PAM hydrogels swell (Gao et al., 2016). However, the SR of PL-CD-4 and PL-CD-5 at pH 11.2 were not much higher compared to that at pH 3.2 and 7.2. This could possibly be explained by that the PL-CD can form more hydrogen bonds which make the hydrogels shrink (Gao et al., 2016; Peng et al., 2014).

The TGA spectra of MBA-5 hydrogel, AM-1 hydrogel and PL-CD-3 hydrogel are presented in Fig. 7. A weight loss (MBA-5:12.8621%; AM-1: 12.1626%; PL-CD-3: 12.9727%) had been observed at the initial stage (below 150 ℃) indicating the loss of the bound water in the hydrogels (Ovalle-Serrano et al., 2020). Then, a weight loss (MBA-5:17.2669%; AM-1: 22.3173%; PL-CD-3: 17.8448%) had been observed from 150 ℃ to 320 ℃, which could be explained by the cleavage of side chain groups and backbone scission (Zhao et al., 2015). The weight loss was different among these samples. Increasing the AM amount can increase the polymerization degree of the side chains which lead to better thermal stability at 320℃. Furthermore, the addition of PL-CD can form more hydrogen bonds which lead to better thermal stability at 320 ℃. Thus, the weight loss of MBA-5 hydrogel, AM-1 hydrogel and PL-CD-3 hydrogel were different at 150-320℃.

The weight of MBA-5 hydrogel, AM-1 hydrogel and PL-CD-3 hydrogel thirdly decreased (MBA-5: 50.4379%; AM-1: 43.9360%; PL-CD-3: 43.3394%) between 320 ℃ to 600 ℃, which may be caused by the degradation of PAM (Peng et al., 2014). MBA-5 hydrogel showed different weigh loss compared to AM-1 hydrogel and PL-CD-3 hydrogel. This could possibly be explained by that the higher dosage of AM in MBA-5 hydrogel lead to lower crosslinking degree which lea to poor thermal stability.

Fig. 8 shows the SEM images of MBA-5 hydrogel 4, AM-1 hydrogel and PL-CD-3 hydrogel at 200 µm and 100 µm. A porous structure can be observed in the MBA-5 hydrogel 4, AM-1 hydrogel and PL-CD-3 hydrogel. The crosslinking formed a honeycomb-like structure and the pore size was small, which could effectively hold the water. The structure of the pores inside the hydrogel can be easily observed in the SEM images. Furthermore, honeycomb-like structure of the MBA-5 hydrogel and AM-1 hydrogel were much bigger and rough compared to the PL-CD-3 hydrogel.

It is well known that the -NH2 groups could interact with the bacterial cell membrane and destroy the normal metabolism of the bacteria, resulting antibacterial properties (Cao et al., 2022; Sarwar et al., 2018; Zhong et al., 2021). The photograph of inhibition zones and the diameter of inhibition zones are presented in Fig. 9 and Table 2. With the increasing of MBA amount the bacterial properties were improved, while with the increasing of AM amount, the bacterial properties decreased. Among the MBA 1–5 hydrogels and AM 1–5 hydrogels, MBA-5 hydrogel (The diameter of the inhibition zone: 13 mm) and AM-1 hydrogel (The diameter of the inhibition zone: 14 mm) showed better antibacterial properties. This can be explained by the changes of SR. The lower SR could promote the encapsulation of the PL-CD into the hydrogels which could promote the antibacterial properties. Furthermore, when increasing the content of PL-CD, the antibacterial activities of the hydrogel first increased and then remained nearly constant. This can be explained by that the increasing of -NH2 groups content could lead to higher bacteriostatic efficiency (Yang et al., 2021). When the PL-CD content exceeded a certain value, the bacteriostatic efficiency remained nearly constant which indicated that the extra additional of PL-CD could not have a significant impact on the antibacterial properties.

The comparation PL-CD-CMX-PAM hydrogel and the hydrogel without PL-CD are presented in Table 3. The hydrogel without PL-CD had a compressive strength of 34.5 KPa, while MBA-5 hydrogel, AM-1 hydrogel and PL-CD-3 hydrogel had the compressive strength of 180.4 KPa, 131.6 KPa and 158.7 KPa. The SR of the hydrogel at water at different pH decreased with the addition of PL-CD. The addition of PL-CD can significantly improve the compressive strength and the SR. Furthermore, the hydrogel had antibacterial properties with the addition of PL-CD. These results suggested that the addition of PL-CD can not only improve the mechanical properties, but also improve the antibacterial properties of the hydrogels.

In this study, a novel PL-CD-CMX-PAM hydrogel with antibacterial property were prepared by free radical polymerization. PL-CD was used as antibacterial agent and nano- reinforcing agent in the carboxymethyl xylan-based hydrogels. The FT-IR confirmed the formation of the PL-CD-CMX-PAM hydrogels. The amount of AM, MBA and PL-CD had the crucial influence on the mechanical properties, SR and antibacterial properties. A porous honeycomb-like structure was clearly shown in the SEM images of the PL-CD-CMX-PAM hydrogels. The TGA analysis showed that the resulting hydrogel had satisfying thermal properties. The addition of PL-CD could significantly improve the antibacterial properties. Furthermore, the mechanical properties first increased and then decreased when the amount of PL-CD increased. The antibacterial properties suggested that the PL-CD-CMX-PAM hydrogels had the potential to be promising materials for biomedical application.

This work was supported by the Foundation (No. KF202106) of Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Science & Technology of Ministry of Education, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences).

Wang Shengdan: Supervision. He Chao: Supervision. Zhang Xiaoran: Supervision. Liu Yushen: Investigation. Hu Zhenhua: Investigation, Resources, Writing – original draft. Fan Xiaotong: Investigation, Resources, Writing – original draft. Xiang Zhouyang: Formal analysis, Supervision. Wang Luliang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that all the authors have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
